abstract: We present Vanast, a unified framework that generates garment-transferred human animation videos directly from a single human image, garment images, and a pose guidance video. Conventional two-stage pipelines treat image-based virtual try-on and pose-driven animation as separate processes, which often results in identity drift, garment distortion, and front-back inconsistency. Our model addresses these issues by performing the entire process in a single unified step to achieve coherent synthesis. To enable this setting, we construct large-scale triplet supervision. Our data generation pipeline includes generating identity-preserving human images in alternative outfits that differ from garment catalog images, capturing full upper and lower garment triplets to overcome the single-garment-posed video pair limitation, and assembling diverse in-the-wild triplets without requiring garment catalog images. We further introduce a Dual Module architecture for video diffusion transformers to stabilize training, preserve pretrained generative quality, and improve garment accuracy, pose adherence, and identity preservation while supporting zero-shot garment interpolation. Together, these contributions allow Vanast to produce high-fidelity, identity-consistent animation across a wide range of garment types.

citations.fullCitation: Z. Jiang, Z. Han, C. Mao, J. Zhang, Y. Pan, and Y. Liu. Vace: All-in-one video creation and editing. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.07598, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is a direct and crucial point of comparison. The Vanast paper positions its 'Dual Module' architecture as an improvement over VACE's 'single auxiliary module' for handling joint conditioning. VACE is also used as a key baseline in both single-stage and two-stage experimental comparisons.
citations.title: Vace: All-in-one video creation and editing

citations.fullCitation: T. Wan, A. Wang, B. Ai, B. Wen, C. Mao, C.-W. Xie, D. Chen, F. Yu, H. Zhao, J. Yang, et al. Wan: Open and advanced large-scale video generative models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.20314, 2025.
citations.justification: This work provides the foundational video diffusion transformer (DiT) backbone upon which the Vanast model is built. The paper explicitly states that it adopts and modifies the architecture from this T2V model, making it a fundamental building block for their proposed method.
citations.title: Wan: Open and advanced large-scale video generative models

citations.fullCitation: H. Li, Y. Li, Y. Yang, J. Cao, Z. Zhu, X. Cheng, and L. Chen. Dispose: Disentangling pose guidance for controllable human image animation. arXiv preprint arXiv:2412.09349, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper represents a state-of-the-art approach for the pose-driven human animation task, which is one of the two core problems Vanast unifies. It is used as a primary component in the two-stage baseline pipelines for experimental comparison, demonstrating its relevance to the animation aspect of Vanast's contributions.
citations.title: Dispose: Disentangling pose guidance for controllable human image animation

citations.fullCitation: Y. Feng, L. Zhang, H. Cao, Y. Chen, X. Feng, J. Cao, Y. Wu, and B. Wang. Omnitry: Virtual try-on anything without masks. arXiv preprint arXiv:2508.13632, 2025.
citations.justification: OmniTry is a state-of-the-art image-based virtual try-on model that Vanast aims to improve upon by creating a unified video-native framework. It is used as a key baseline in the quantitative comparisons, representing the performance of the separate try-on stage that Vanast's single-stage approach replaces.
citations.title: Omnitry: Virtual try-on anything without masks

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: Vanast: Virtual Try-On with Human Image Animation via Synthetic Triplet Supervision

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The authors of the paper "Vanast: Virtual Try-On with Human Image Animation via Synthetic Triplet Supervision" are Hyunsoo Cha, Wonjung Woo, Byungjun Kim, and Hanbyul Joo. All authors are affiliated with Seoul National University.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The field of computer vision has seen significant advancements in human image synthesis, particularly in virtual try-on (VTON) and human animation. Historically, these tasks have been approached as separate problems, often leading to multi-stage pipelines.

**Virtual Try-On (VTON)**
Early VTON methods relied on geometry-driven clothing warping, combining human and clothing parsing with geometric alignment and appearance blending. These approaches were sensitive to pose variations, occlusions, and non-rigid garment deformations. More recent advancements, particularly with diffusion models, have shifted towards learned correspondence priors. Mask-conditioned dual-UNet architectures employ segmentation masks for spatial control, while transformer-based diffusion models infer garment-body correspondences implicitly, enhancing generalization. However, these VTON methods are fundamentally image-centric and typically exhibit temporal flickering and identity inconsistencies when applied to video sequences.

**Diffusion-based Human Animation**
Parallel to VTON, diffusion models have also driven progress in human and portrait animation, focusing on generating coherent motion from a single reference image. Models like Animate Anyone and Champ extend 2D UNet architectures with temporal attention layers, leveraging large-scale 2D image priors to generate temporally consistent animation videos. Advanced avatar animation systems use video diffusion frameworks for robust pose transfer and identity preservation, even under complex motions. ControlNet-based conditioning has further enabled accurate, pose-driven control. Despite these capabilities, existing animation models generally lack mechanisms for garment transfer, preventing their direct use in virtual try-on animation scenarios where a separate garment input is required.

**Subject-driven Image and Video Generation**
A related area is subject-driven generation, where identity is conditioned while composing additional attributes. Diffusion Transformer-based models for image synthesis emphasize identity preservation and compositional control, and can be adapted for localized inpainting in VTON. However, they still require a separate animation stage for temporal coherence. Recent diffusion-based models like VACE generate videos directly from a subject, incorporating text prompts to control actions and background. VACE's unified architecture can handle various tasks, including reference-to-video generation and pose-conditioned video-to-video synthesis, which can be adapted for pose-controlled virtual try-on. However, jointly conditioning pose, garment, and human image through a single auxiliary module can lead to difficulties in preserving fine garment details or synthesizing accurate pose motion.

The presented work, Vanast, positions itself at the intersection of these domains. It addresses the limitations of existing two-stage pipelines by proposing a unified, single-stage framework for virtual try-on with human image animation. This approach aims to overcome the issues of identity drift, garment distortion, front-back inconsistency, and computational inefficiency inherent in separate image VTON and video animation models. Furthermore, it tackles the critical challenge of constructing appropriate training data, which no existing public dataset provides, to enable this unified learning paradigm.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop a unified, single-stage framework, named Vanast, capable of generating realistic garment-transferred human animation videos directly from a single human image, one or more garment images, and a pose guidance video. This framework aims to synthesize a coherent video of a person wearing a target garment and performing actions dictated by a pose video, while preserving the person's identity.

This objective is motivated by several limitations observed in conventional two-stage pipelines for virtual try-on animation:

*   **Artifact Accumulation and Inconsistency:** When image-based virtual try-on models and pose-driven video generation models are applied sequentially, discrepancies between their respective training distributions often lead to compounding artifacts. This manifests as identity drift, where the person's identity subtly changes across frames, and garment distortion, where the transferred garment's appearance becomes inconsistent or unrealistic over time.
*   **Front-Back Inconsistency:** Standard video animation models, when operating from a single static image, lack the necessary information to synthesize consistent appearances for garments with distinct front-back geometries across diverse viewpoints. A two-stage pipeline exacerbates this issue, as the image-based try-on might only provide a frontal view, leading to inconsistent garment representation during animation.
*   **Computational Inefficiency:** Decomposing the task into two separate models (image try-on followed by video animation) is computationally intensive, requiring sequential processing and potentially redundant computations. A unified approach could offer improved efficiency.
*   **Lack of Suitable Training Data:** A significant barrier to developing a single-stage model is the absence of large-scale triplet supervision datasets. Such a dataset would require a human image (of a person wearing an *alternative* outfit), target garment images, and a corresponding RGB video of the same person moving while wearing the target garment. Existing datasets either provide garment-video pairs where the human image wears the *same* clothing as in the video (which prioritizes motion reenactment over garment transfer), or they lack diverse garment types and in-the-wild scenarios.
*   **Challenges in Conditioning:** Training video diffusion transformers with multiple conditioning signals (human image, garment images, pose video) presents difficulties. Simple concatenation or fusion of inputs can hinder fast convergence and destabilize optimization. Dedicated context modules, while an alternative, often struggle to uniformly propagate all constraints, leading to certain conditions being underrepresented or ignored, thereby compromising garment accuracy, pose adherence, or identity preservation.

By addressing these motivations, Vanast aims to achieve coherent synthesis, improved fidelity in garment transfer and identity preservation, accurate pose adherence, and enhanced computational efficiency compared to prior multi-stage approaches.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The Vanast framework is designed as a single-stage video diffusion model that generates human animation with garment transfer. It takes target garment images (G), a target human image (I_G') wearing an arbitrary garment different from G, a motion guidance video (K), and an optional text prompt (T) as input. The output is an F-frame animation sequence where the person wears the target garments and performs the specified motion.

**4.1. Synthetic Triplet Dataset Generation**
A core contribution of Vanast is its pipeline for constructing large-scale, high-quality triplet datasets (I_G', G, V_G) for training, as no suitable public datasets exist.

*   **Synthesizing I_G' from (G, V_G):**
    *   **Frame Selection and Cropping:** From online shopping videos (V_G), a suitable candidate frame (I_G) is selected using a Vision-Language Model (VLM, Qwen2.5-VL). Criteria for selection include clear, unoccluded, near-frontal face, open eyes, and high image quality. Adaptive cropping is applied to support various human image scales.
    *   **Inpainting Mask Construction:** A critical step is to construct an inpainting mask (M_inpaint) that reflects an *expected* garment region rather than the original garment's silhouette. This prevents the inpainting model from simply preserving the existing garment shape. This is achieved by using a text-to-image model (SDXL) to synthesize an auxiliary image with the same pose as I_G but featuring an arbitrary garment and identity, then extracting a garment mask from this synthesized image using a segmentation model.
    *   **Garment Synthesis:** Using the selected I_G and the constructed M_inpaint, a pre-trained diffusion inpainting model (FLUX) generates I_G', depicting the same person wearing a different garment from the same category as G. Text prompts for inpainting are randomly composed from a pool of garment types and colors using a Large Language Model (LLM, ChatGPT), with gender consistency ensured by VLM classification.

*   **Synthesizing (I_G', G) from V_G (In-the-Wild):**
    *   To increase diversity in pose and background, triplets are also collected from in-the-wild video datasets. The pipeline for constructing I_G' remains the same.
    *   For synthesizing G (the garment image, which is absent in in-the-wild videos), a representative frame is selected from V_G using VLM-evaluated criteria: high frontal scores, full-body visibility, sharpness, minimal occlusion, lighting/contrast, and composition.
    *   From this selected frame, an upper-clothing mask is extracted using a segmentation model. A garment-highlighted image is generated by filling the background outside the mask with white. Random translation is applied to prevent bias from garment position. A VLM then validates the segmented garment to filter unstable segmentations.

*   **Studio-Captured Dataset:** A custom, high-quality studio dataset is captured to specifically support scenarios involving multiple garments (e.g., upper and lower garments combined).

**4.2. Model Architecture: Dual Modules**
Vanast employs a Dual Module architecture built upon a pre-trained Video Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone (Wan). This design aims to overcome the challenges of integrating multiple conditioning signals in a single module.

*   **Distributed and Cascaded Structure:** The architecture divides the conditioning into two specialized, trainable modules that share parts of the DiT block structure:
    *   **Human Animation Module (HAM):** Dedicated to generating human animation using conditioned human image (I_G') and pose video (K) information.
    *   **Garment Transfer Module (GTM):** Responsible for garment transfer using garment images (G).
*   **Progressive Context Integration:** This distributed and cascaded design allows for progressive integration of contextual information across multiple levels of the representation space, intending to achieve richer conditioning effects than single-point injection.
*   **Frozen Backbone:** The backbone DiT is kept frozen during training, with only the HAM and GTM modules being optimized. This approach aims to preserve the pretrained generative quality of the DiT backbone while enabling fast and stable convergence.
*   **Feature Integration:** Hidden states (h_l) from the backbone DiT are integrated with outputs from HAM and GTM at specific transformer blocks (l = 2k), with scalar weights (α=0.5, β=0.5) controlling their relative contributions.

**4.3. Tokenization**
Inputs (I_G', G, K) are converted into latent representations using a pre-trained VAE encoder (E_VAE).
*   For HAM, the encoded latents of I_G' (z_H) and K (z_P) are concatenated frame-wise along the temporal dimension to form a motion-conditioned appearance context.
*   For GTM, the encoded latent of G (z_G) is used, with a zero tensor appended to match the temporal dimension of HAM.
*   A 3D convolutional projection layer then maps these concatenated latent volumes into token embeddings for the Dual Modules.

**4.4. Garment Interpolation**
The modular conditioning structure of Vanast enables zero-shot garment interpolation. For two garments (G_A, G_B) of the same category, their respective GTM outputs are computed. A weighted summation of these representations, controlled by an interpolation ratio γ ∈ [0, 1], allows the model to produce smooth transitions between garment styles without additional fine-tuning.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research presents both quantitative and qualitative evaluations of Vanast, demonstrating its performance against various baselines and showcasing its capabilities.

**5.1. Quantitative Comparisons**
Vanast was quantitatively compared against 16 baseline combinations, constructed from state-of-the-art image virtual try-on models (OOTDiffusion, CatVTON, OmniTry, Any2AnyTryon), subject-to-image generation models (VisualCloze, MOSAIC, UNO), and human animation models (StableAnimator, DisPose). Additionally, VACE, a diffusion transformer-based model for subject-driven video generation, was evaluated in both single-stage and two-stage configurations.

Experiments were conducted on two distinct datasets: the Internet dataset (sourced from public shopping-mall websites) and the ViViD dataset (an official test split where I_G' was generated using image outpainting). Evaluation metrics included L1 distance, PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS (perceptual similarity), FID (perceptual realism), and VFID (temporal consistency using I3D and ResNeXt).

*   **Superior Performance:** Vanast consistently achieved the best performance across nearly all metrics on both the Internet and ViViD datasets, outperforming all baseline combinations. This included lower L1 distances, LPIPS scores, FID scores, and VFID scores, along with higher PSNR values. For SSIM, Vanast's score was comparable to the best-performing baselines.
*   **VACE Comparison:** Vanast also demonstrated superior performance compared to both the 1-stage and 2-stage variants of VACE, indicating the effectiveness of its specialized Dual Module architecture in handling multiple conditioning signals for the virtual try-on animation task.

**5.2. Qualitative Comparisons**
Qualitative results visually supported the quantitative findings. Figures in the paper illustrate that Vanast produces:

*   **Accurate Pose Following and Garment Transfer:** The generated animations exhibit precise adherence to the guidance pose while accurately transferring the target garment details.
*   **Identity Preservation:** The identity of the person from the input human image is faithfully maintained throughout the animated sequence.
*   **High Fidelity:** The overall visual quality and realism of the synthesized videos are higher compared to the baselines, closely resembling the ground truth.

**5.3. Ablation Study**
An ablation study was conducted to evaluate the contributions of key architectural components and the effectiveness of the synthetic triplet dataset generation.

*   **Dual Module Architecture:**
    *   A "Single Module" baseline (single trainable module for all conditions) failed to accurately control pose conditions, resulting in poorer overall metrics.
    *   A "Backbone-LoRA" baseline (concatenating all inputs directly to the frozen T2V backbone and fine-tuning with LoRA) struggled with accurate garment transfer.
    *   Vanast, with its Dual Module architecture, yielded the best performance, confirming its effectiveness in balancing and propagating multiple conditions.
*   **Synthetic Human Image (I_G'):**
    *   The "w/o SynthHuman" variant (trained without generating I_G', relying on a frame from V_G with the same garment I_G) also showed degraded performance in garment transfer. This finding highlights the importance of using an identity-preserving human image with an *alternative* garment (I_G') during training to compel the model to learn garment transfer rather than simply animating existing clothing.

**5.4. Applications and Capabilities**
Vanast demonstrates several practical capabilities:

*   **Garment Interpolation:** The model can generate human animation videos where garments are interpolated between two input garment styles (G_A, G_B) in a zero-shot manner, producing smooth and natural transitions for both upper and lower garments.
*   **Multiple Garment Transfer:** Vanast supports simultaneous transfer of multiple garments (e.g., an upper garment and a lower garment) in a zero-shot setting. The generated animations preserve details of both garments while maintaining strong identity consistency.
*   **In-the-wild Garment Transfer:** Benefiting from the in-the-wild dataset and synthetic triplet generation, the model successfully performs garment transfer even from unconstrained, "in-the-wild" garment images, despite potential pose mismatches between the garment image and the human animation, while maintaining temporal coherence.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The Vanast framework represents an advancement in the field of virtual try-on and human image animation, addressing several critical limitations of existing approaches. Its significance and potential impact stem from its unified architecture, innovative data generation pipeline, and expanded practical capabilities.

**Unified Framework:** The development of a single-stage model for garment-transferred human animation directly addresses the inefficiencies and artifact generation inherent in traditional two-stage pipelines. By synthesizing the entire process coherently, Vanast minimizes issues like identity drift, garment distortion, and front-back inconsistency, leading to more realistic and temporally stable video outputs. This unification offers a more streamlined and computationally efficient solution compared to chaining separate image-based VTON and video animation models.

**Data Generation Innovation:** A major bottleneck in developing robust virtual try-on animation models has been the scarcity of suitable training data. Vanast's scalable pipeline for synthetic triplet supervision is a significant contribution.
*   The method for generating identity-preserving human images in *alternative outfits* (I_G') is crucial. This technique ensures that the model learns to perform garment transfer rather than simply reenacting motion with pre-existing clothing, a common pitfall if using frames from videos where the person already wears the target garment.
*   The ability to construct triplets from diverse *in-the-wild videos* without requiring paired catalog images greatly expands the variability of training data in terms of background, lighting, and garment styles. This enhances the model's robustness and generalization to real-world scenarios.
*   The capture of *full upper and lower garment triplets* overcomes the limitations of online sources that often feature only single garment categories.
These data generation strategies are fundamental to training a unified model effectively and open avenues for future research in synthetic data creation for complex vision tasks.

**Architectural Advancement:** The proposed Dual Module architecture for video Diffusion Transformers (DiT) offers an effective solution for integrating multiple, distinct conditioning signals (human identity, garment information, pose guidance) without sacrificing generative quality or encountering convergence issues. By freezing the backbone and optimizing specialized modules, Vanast achieves fast and stable training while ensuring that each conditioning signal is faithfully reflected in the output. This modular design provides a blueprint for managing complex multi-modal inputs in other generative video tasks.

**Expanded Practical Capabilities:** Vanast's support for zero-shot garment interpolation and multiple-garment transfer without additional fine-tuning significantly enhances its practical utility.
*   **Garment Interpolation** allows for the exploration of diverse styles and customized garment designs, moving beyond discrete catalog items.
*   **Multiple-Garment Transfer** enables the creation of complete outfits, which is essential for realistic fashion applications.
*   The model's robustness to *in-the-wild garment images* further broadens its applicability to user-generated content or less controlled environments.

**Potential Impact:** The capabilities demonstrated by Vanast have broad implications across several industries:
*   **E-commerce and Fashion:** Revolutionizing online shopping by providing highly realistic and personalized virtual try-on experiences, potentially reducing return rates, enhancing customer satisfaction, and enabling more interactive product visualization.
*   **Content Creation:** Empowering creators in digital media, gaming, and entertainment to generate dynamic virtual characters with custom attire for virtual influencers, personalized avatars, and animated narratives.
*   **Fashion Design:** Offering designers a tool for rapid prototyping and visualization of new clothing lines on animated human figures, accelerating the design process and reducing the need for physical samples.
*   **General Human-Centric Video Generation:** Advancing the state-of-the-art in controllable human video synthesis, contributing to broader applications in augmented reality, virtual reality, and human-computer interaction.

In summary, Vanast contributes a unified, high-fidelity solution to a challenging problem, pushing the boundaries of generative AI for human-centric applications and establishing new benchmarks in virtual try-on animation.
overview:
## Virtual Try-On and Animation in a Single Stage

Virtual try-on (VTON) technology has evolved from simple image overlays to sophisticated generative models that can realistically drape garments onto diverse human bodies. However, a significant gap remains between static image try-on and dynamic video animation. Standard approaches typically treat these as two separate problems: first, an image-based model performs the garment transfer, and then a second video-generation model animates that static image. This two-stage pipeline often suffers from compounding errors, leading to "identity drift" where the person's face changes during movement, or "garment distortion" where clothing flickers or loses its structure across frames.

![Vanast Framework Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04934v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: The Vanast framework employs a single-stage video diffusion architecture. It integrates a Human Animation Module (HAM) and a Garment Transfer Module (GTM) with a frozen Video Diffusion Transformer backbone to generate temporally consistent, garment-transferred human animations from a single human image and target garment.*

Vanast addresses these issues by proposing a unified, single-stage framework that simultaneously handles garment transfer and human animation. Built upon a pre-trained Video Diffusion Transformer (DiT), the model generates a coherent video sequence directly from a target human image, one or more garment images, and a pose guidance video. By processing all constraints within a single architecture, the system maintains better temporal consistency and preserves fine details of both the person's identity and the garment's texture.

## The Challenge of Supervision: Synthetic Triplet Generation

The primary obstacle in training a single-stage model for virtual try-on animation is the lack of suitable data. A model needs "triplet" supervision to learn effectively. This triplet consists of:
1.  **A human image** $(I_G')$ of a person wearing an arbitrary, different outfit.
2.  **Target garment images** ($G$) that the person should "change into."
3.  **A ground-truth video** ($V_G$) of the *same* person wearing the *target* garments ($G$) while performing a specific motion.

Existing datasets usually provide either paired images (human and garment) or videos of people moving in their own clothes. They rarely provide a video of a person wearing a specific garment alongside an image of that same person in a different outfit. To solve this, the researchers developed an automated pipeline to synthesize these triplets from existing shopping videos and "in-the-wild" footage.

### Synthesizing the Human Reference
To create $(I_G', G, V_G)$, the system starts with a high-quality video $V_G$ where the person is already wearing the target garment $G$. The challenge is creating $I_G'$—a static image of that person wearing *something else*. If the model were trained using an image of the person already wearing $G$, it would simply learn to copy the clothes rather than transfer them.

The pipeline uses a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to select the best "identity" frame from the video, ensuring the face is clear and unoccluded. It then uses a text-to-image diffusion model (like FLUX) to "inpaint" new clothes onto that person. Crucially, the inpainting mask is not just the original clothes' shape; the system synthesizes an auxiliary "target" garment shape to ensure the new clothes in $I_G'$ have a different silhouette than the original $G$. This forces the model to learn the actual transfer process $I_G' \rightarrow V_G \text{ given } G$ during training.

### In-the-Wild and Multi-Garment Data
The researchers also extended this to "in-the-wild" videos where no separate catalog image of the garment exists. They used VLMs to automatically extract and segment the cleanest view of the garment from the video to serve as the reference $G$. Additionally, they captured a dedicated studio dataset to support complex scenarios like transferring both upper and lower garments simultaneously, a feature many current VTON models struggle with.

## Architectural Innovation: The Dual Module Design

Integrating multiple, often conflicting, conditioning signals—pose, identity, and garment details—into a single video diffusion model is technically difficult. If all inputs are simply concatenated, the model might prioritize one (like the pose) while ignoring the fine details of the garment or losing the person's identity.

![Vanast Architecture and Conditioning](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04934v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The architecture utilizes distributed modules. The Human Animation Module (HAM) processes pose and identity, while the Garment Transfer Module (GTM) focuses on garment features. These outputs are integrated into a frozen pre-trained backbone.*

Vanast utilizes a "Dual Module" architecture. Instead of modifying the core weights of a massive pre-trained Video Diffusion Transformer (which is kept frozen to preserve its generative quality), the researchers added two specialized, trainable components:

1.  **Human Animation Module (HAM):** This module focuses on the temporal and structural aspects. It takes the human reference image $I_G'$ and the pose video $K$ as input. It ensures the generated person looks like the reference and follows the target motion.
2.  **Garment Transfer Module (GTM):** This module is dedicated to the garment $G$. It extracts high-frequency details (textures, patterns, logos) and ensures they are correctly mapped onto the human body as it moves.

These modules are "distributed and cascaded." Their outputs are added to the hidden states of the frozen backbone at specific intervals. For example, at every other transformer block $l$, the integrated feature is calculated as:

$$
h_l = h_{l-1} + \alpha \cdot \text{HAM}_k(h_{l-1}) + \beta \cdot \text{GTM}_k(h_{l-1})
$$

where $l = 2k$ represents the specific block index. The researchers found that using fixed weights like $\alpha = 0.5$ and $\beta = 0.5$ provides a stable balance between following the motion and preserving the garment's appearance.

## Advanced Features: Interpolation and Multi-Garment Support

The modular nature of this architecture allows for capabilities that go beyond simple "copy-paste" garment transfer.

### Zero-Shot Garment Interpolation
Because the garment information is processed through a dedicated GTM, the model can perform "garment interpolation." If a user provides two different garments $(G_A, G_B)$, the system can mathematically blend their representations in the latent space. By adjusting an interpolation ratio $\gamma \in [0, 1]$, the model generates an animation where the person wears a "hybrid" garment that smoothly transitions between the two styles.

$$
H_{interp} = (1 - \gamma) H_A + \gamma H_B
$$

This occurs in a "zero-shot" manner, meaning the model does not need to be retrained or fine-tuned to understand how to blend styles; it emerges naturally from the modular feature integration.

![Garment Interpolation Results](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04934v1/x9.png)
*Figure 3: Demonstration of zero-shot garment interpolation. By varying the ratio $\gamma$, the model generates smooth transitions between two different garment styles while maintaining motion and identity.*

### Full Outfit Transfer
While many virtual try-on systems are limited to just a single T-shirt or a dress, Vanast's training on specialized multi-garment datasets allows it to handle full outfits. It can simultaneously condition on an upper garment (e.g., a jacket) and a lower garment (e.g., jeans), maintaining the correct layering and interaction between them even during complex movements.

## Evaluation and Performance

The researchers evaluated Vanast against a rigorous set of 16 baseline combinations. These included state-of-the-art image-based VTON models (like OOTDiffusion and CatVTON) paired with human animation models (like StableAnimator). They also compared it against VACE, a recent diffusion-based model designed for subject-driven video generation.

### Quantitative Metrics
The model was tested on two datasets: "Internet" (sourced from shopping malls) and "ViViD" (a standard benchmark). The evaluation used several key metrics:
*   **LPIPS and SSIM:** To measure how closely the generated frames match the ground truth in terms of visual structure and perception.
*   **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance):** To assess the overall realism and quality of the generated images.
*   **VFID (Video FID):** Specifically designed to measure temporal consistency and motion fluidity in videos.

In almost every category, Vanast outperformed the multi-stage baselines. Notably, the VFID scores were significantly lower than two-stage methods, confirming that the unified approach effectively eliminates the flickering and jitter common in chained models.

![Qualitative Comparison vs Baselines](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04934v1/x3.png)
*Figure 4: Qualitative comparison showing Vanast's superior ability to preserve identity and garment details compared to existing 1-stage and 2-stage baselines.*

### Qualitative Superiority
Visually, the differences are striking. Two-stage models often struggle when the person turns around; since the initial image try-on only provides a front view, the animation stage has to "guess" what the back of the garment looks like, often leading to blurred textures or sudden changes in color. Vanast, having been trained on full video sequences with the Dual Module architecture, maintains a consistent 360-degree representation of the garment.

## Significance for the Future of Digital Fashion

The development of Vanast marks a move toward more integrated and robust human-centric video generation. By solving the data scarcity problem through synthetic triplet supervision and addressing the conditioning problem through the Dual Module architecture, the research provides a framework for high-fidelity virtual experiences.

The potential impacts are diverse:
*   **E-commerce:** Customers could see themselves moving in clothes before buying, seeing how a dress flows or how a jacket fits during a walk.
*   **Content Creation:** Digital creators can generate high-quality animations for virtual influencers or avatars with specific, detailed clothing without needing expensive motion capture or 3D modeling.
*   **AI Research:** The synthetic data pipeline demonstrates how to train complex video models when real paired data is impossible to collect at scale.

By unifying try-on and animation, Vanast demonstrates that the most effective way to handle complex human-garment interactions is not through a sequence of specialized models, but through a single, well-conditioned generative process that understands both form and motion simultaneously.

keyInsights: Generating an identity-preserving human image wearing an *alternative* outfit (I_G') is critical for training, forcing the model to learn garment transfer rather than merely re-enacting motion with existing clothing.

A distributed and cascaded Dual Module architecture effectively balances and propagates multiple, distinct conditioning signals (human identity, garment, pose) within a video diffusion transformer, leading to more stable and accurate generation.

Freezing the pre-trained Video Diffusion Transformer backbone and optimizing only specialized conditioning modules allows for fast, stable convergence while preserving the backbone's high generative quality.
originalProblem: Existing two-stage pipelines for virtual try-on animation suffer from accumulating artifacts, identity drift, garment distortion, and front-back inconsistencies, alongside computational inefficiencies.
A lack of large-scale, high-quality triplet supervision datasets (human image in alternative outfit, target garment, pose video) hinders the development of unified, single-stage models.

Integrating multiple, distinct conditioning signals (human identity, garment information, pose guidance) into a single video diffusion transformer is challenging, often leading to unstable optimization or compromised output quality.
results: Vanast consistently outperformed 16 baseline combinations, including state-of-the-art image VTON, subject-to-image generation, human animation models, and VACE variants, across most quantitative metrics (L1, LPIPS, FID, VFID, PSNR) on both Internet and ViViD datasets.

Qualitative evaluations show Vanast produces animations with accurate pose following, precise garment transfer, faithful identity preservation, and higher overall visual fidelity compared to baselines.
The model demonstrates zero-shot garment interpolation, multiple-garment transfer capabilities, and robust transfer from unconstrained in-the-wild garment images, maintaining temporal coherence.
solution: Developed Vanast, a single-stage video diffusion model that directly generates garment-transferred human animation videos from a human image, garment images, and a pose guidance video.
Designed a scalable pipeline to construct large-scale synthetic triplet datasets, crucially generating human images in *alternative* outfits (I_G') to compel the model to learn true garment transfer.

Implemented a Dual Module architecture built upon a frozen Video Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone, distributing conditioning into specialized Human Animation and Garment Transfer modules for progressive context integration.
summary.summary: The Vanast framework from Seoul National University introduces a single-stage video diffusion model for virtual try-on with human image animation, generating realistic and temporally consistent videos of a person wearing new garments and performing specified motions. The approach leverages a novel synthetic triplet supervision dataset and a dual-module architecture to overcome limitations of traditional multi-stage pipelines, achieving superior quantitative and qualitative results across various metrics.

title: Vanast: Virtual Try-On with Human Image Animation via Synthetic Triplet Supervision
